blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following relation:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: This equation expresses the total differential in terms of the partial derivatives with respect to x and y.

blocks.text:
The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of change, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$. 

Thus:

blocks.latex: \Delta z = \frac{\Delta z}{\Delta x} \Delta x + \frac{\Delta z}{\Delta y} \Delta y
blocks.description: This equation relates the change in z to the changes in x and y.

blocks.text:
The change in $z$ due to the change in $x$ can be measured by the product of the change in $x$ multiplied by the rate at which it is changing with respect to $z$; and this fact can be better illustrated by referring to a diagram (Fig. 21). 

Let P be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let P move to a new position Q near to P. The change of position is modeled by a vector that points from P to Q on the surface (y being kept constant).
